schools.summary: 'Carnal' (sarkinois, 'men of flesh,' distinct from the worse sarkikos of v3); 'babes in Christ' — weak in grace though eminent in gifts (Wesley); the sanctified are so only in part (Henry, 2 Pet 3:18).
Chrysostom: having overturned worldly philosophy, Paul comes to a new argument, meeting their conceit.

'Milk' the elementary doctrines (new birth, repentance, faith; cf Heb 5:12-14), 'meat' the deeper; suit the doctrine to the hearer (Wesley); the sting in 'neither yet now are ye able' — persisting immaturity.
Calvin: the difference is in manner and form, not substance — the same Christ preached, adapted to capacity.

'Carnal' (sarkikos, living according to the flesh); the three faults — envying (heart), strife (words), divisions (deeds); to 'walk as men' is to live by the world's principles; their quarrels the proof of carnality, not gifts the proof of spirituality.
Calvin: while the flesh prevails, the wisdom of God finds no admittance.

'I am of Paul / of Apollos' (cf 1 Cor 1:12) — Paul and Apollos taught the same gospel, so preferring one was a clannish preference of persons, the essence of carnal division; the Spirit allows no party zeal, even for the truest teacher (Wesley).

Not 'who' (tis) but 'what' (ti) — Paul and Apollos are merely 'ministers' (diakonoi), servants through whom they believed, each as the Lord gave; on a level, none the head of a sect; Paul names himself to avoid envy (Calvin).
Paul 'planted' at Corinth (Acts 18:1), Apollos 'watered' (Acts 18:24), 'but God gave the increase' (imperfect, kept on giving) — complementary works, not rival; the growth God's sole prerogative.
Calvin: the word and preaching are necessary means, yet powerless without the divine blessing that alone gives life.

The ministers are 'nothing' (comparatively, not absolutely) beside the God who gives the increase; 'God is the whole and we are not anything' — all the glory his alone, leaving no room for glorying in men.
Calvin: Paul depreciates the ministers not absolutely but in respect of the glory due to God.

Planter and waterer 'are one' in cause and aim (not rivals), yet 'each shall receive his own reward according to his labour' — unity of purpose with individuality of accountability; reward from God, by labour, not from men by fame.

The thrice-repeated 'God's' — 'God's fellow-workers' (theou synergoi, 2 Cor 6:1), 'God's husbandry' (field), 'God's building'; God lets his servants labour with him; the church and its teachers alike belong to God.
Calvin: the best argument — the reward is sure because the work is the Lord's.

'According to the grace of God' guards against self-laudation (Rom 12:3); the 'wise master-builder' (sophos architekton) laid the foundation; the warning 'take heed how he buildeth' to all who teach after him.
Henry: there may be bad building on a good foundation — let every man take heed.
'Other foundation' (themelion allon, masculine — Christ in mind) can no man lay; the one foundation laid once for all is Jesus Christ; all other supposed foundations sandy and false.
Calvin: Christ the only foundation of the Church — the Corinthians, tickled with novelty, must be brought back to him alone.

Three durable (gold, silver, marble) and three perishable (wood, hay, stubble) — palace vs mud hut; sound doctrine and faithful work vs error and human invention; chosen for their behaviour in fire, anticipating the test.
'The day' the day of judgment (1 Thess 5:4), revealing the hidden; the work 'revealed in fire' (2 Thess 1:8; Mal 3:1-3) and tried 'of what sort it is' — quality judged then, not appearance now.

If the work 'abide' the fire — sound doctrine and true labour surviving — 'he shall receive a reward' (a special degree of glory, Wesley); the fire preserves and approves the precious; nothing truly built on Christ is lost.
His work 'burned down' (katakaesetai), he 'suffers loss' (reward forfeited), yet 'himself saved, so as through fire' — narrowly, like one snatched from a burning house; the whole metaphorical.

debates.question: Does 'saved, yet so as by fire' teach purgatory?

sides.position: No — the fire is the metaphorical testing of the work at judgment, not a purging of the soul; the Roman doctrine wrongly built on it

schools.summary: 'A temple of God' (naos, the inner sanctuary, not hieron) where the Spirit makes his home — as the Shechinah filled the temple, so the Spirit indwells the church; a consecrated, holy building.
Calvin: Paul turns from the teachers to the people — take care you be not defiled.

themes: ye are the temple of God

schools.summary: The same verb in both clauses (phtheirei) — 'destroy the temple... him will God destroy,' exact retribution; the sin grave because its object is holy; aimed at the false teachers and schismatics marring God's temple.
Calvin: a dreadful threat against those who corrupt the church, intruding to rule in God's place.

'Let no man deceive himself' (some were); the self-wise must 'become a fool' — renounce worldly wisdom and embrace the gospel the world counts foolish — to be truly wise; humility the remedy for conceit (Henry).
Calvin: this touches the true sore — the whole trouble sprang from their being wise in their own conceit.

themes: become a fool to be wise

schools.summary: 'The wisdom of this world is foolishness with God' — not science/learning but the world's wisdom about religion; Scripture (Job 5:13) confirms God 'taketh the wise in their own craftiness,' their cleverness becoming their snare.

A second proof from Ps 94:11 (Paul changing 'men' to 'wise' for the context): the Lord knows the 'thoughts/reasonings' (dialogismous) of the wise are 'vain' (useless, futile) — testimony piled on testimony against boasted wisdom.

themes: 'the thoughts of the wise are vain'

schools.summary: 'Let no man glory in men' — the conclusion from the self-conceit condemned; but the surprising ground is 'all things are yours' — to call oneself by a man's name is to degrade oneself, claiming a part when one owns the whole.
Calvin: God takes away from mankind universally every ground of glorying.

The sweeping catalogue — Paul, Apollos, Cephas (all the teachers), the world, life, death, things present and to come — all belong to the believer; the bold leap from Peter to the world enlarging the thought (Wesley); why glory in one when you possess all?

The ascending chain — all things yours, ye are Christ's, Christ is God's; the riches held in subordination, flowing from union with Christ, who refers all to God; 'Christ is God's' guards against making even his name a faction.
Calvin: Christ's being 'God's' concerns his humanity and mediation (Phil 2:7-8), by which he joins us to the Father.
